items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Why ARF and RHD Persist in LMICs Despite Global Decline
items.content: ARF and RHD are diseases of poverty, historically declining in high-income countries due to improved living conditions and medical care. However, they persist in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) due to inadequate resources and lack of coordinated control programs.
keyPoints: ARF and RHD were once common globally but declined with better hygiene and antibiotics in industrialized nations.
>40 million people worldwide are affected by RHD, leading to >300,000 deaths annually.
95% of ARF cases and RHD deaths occur in developing countries.
High burdens exist in sub-Saharan Africa, Pacific nations, Australasia, China, South and Central Asia.
Few LMICs have coordinated RHD control programs despite their cost-effectiveness.
In 2018, WHO adopted a Global Resolution to address these diseases.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: The Impact of Socioeconomic Factors on ARF and RHD Prevalence
items.content: Socioeconomic disparities contribute significantly to the prevalence of ARF and RHD, with LMICs facing higher burdens due to lack of resources for prevention and control.
keyPoints: ARF and RHD are linked to poverty and inadequate living conditions.
Improved housing and hygiene reduced transmission in industrialized nations.
LMICs continue to face high incidence rates without adequate interventions.
RHD is the leading cause of acquired heart disease in children in LMICs.
International efforts, like WHO's Global Resolution, aim to mobilize resources for control.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | ORGANISM FACTORS | HOST FACTORS
items.title: Age Distribution and Recurrence Pattern of ARF vs. RHD
items.content: Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF) primarily affects children aged 5–14 years, with initial episodes becoming less common in older adolescents and young adults. However, recurrent episodes remain relatively frequent in these age groups. In contrast, Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD) peaks between ages 25 and 40.
keyPoints: ARF is mainly a disease of children aged 5–14 years
Initial ARF episodes are less common in persons >30 years
Recurrent ARF remains relatively common in adolescents and young adults
RHD prevalence peaks between ages 25 and 40
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · EPIDEMIOLOGY | ORGANISM FACTORS | HOST FACTORS

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | ORGANISM FACTORS | HOST FACTORS
items.title: Host Susceptibility to ARF: Genetic Factors and Concordance Rates
items.content: Approximately 3–6% of any population is susceptible to ARF, with familial clustering and high concordance in monozygotic twins indicating genetic predisposition. Studies show a 44% concordance rate for chorea in monozygotic twins compared to 12% in dizygotic twins, highlighting inherited susceptibility.
keyPoints: ~3–6% of any population may be susceptible to ARF
Concordance rates: 44% in monozygotic twins vs. 12% in dizygotic twins for chorea
Heritability estimated at 60%
Genetic associations include IGHV4-61'02, complement factor H, and various HLA alleles
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · EPIDEMIOLOGY | ORGANISM FACTORS | HOST FACTORS

items.subtopic: THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT
items.title: Understanding the Pathogenesis of Rheumatic Fever through Molecular Mimicry
items.content: Rheumatic fever pathogenesis involves molecular mimicry, where an immune response to streptococcal antigens (mainly M protein) mistakenly targets human tissues. This leads to cross-reactive antibodies binding to endothelial cells on heart valves, activating adhesion molecules and recruiting lymphocytes, causing tissue damage.
keyPoints: Molecular mimicry is the primary mechanism in rheumatic fever pathogenesis.
Antigen processing by innate immune system presents streptococcal antigens to T cells.
Cross-reactive antibodies bind to endothelial cells on heart valves, leading to activation of VCAM-1 and recruitment of lymphocytes.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT
items.title: Clinical Features and Latent Period of Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF)
items.content: There is a latent period of approximately 3 weeks between group A streptococcal infection and the onset of ARF symptoms. Common clinical features include polyarthritis (60–75%) and carditis (50–75%), while chorea can follow prolonged latent periods up to 6 months.
keyPoints: Latent period for ARF is ~3 weeks (1–5 weeks).
Polyarthritis occurs in 60–75% of cases.
Carditis is present in 50–75% of cases.
Chorea may follow latent periods up to 6 months.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT
items.title: Heart Involvement and Progression to Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD)
items.content: Up to 75% of ARF patients progress to RHD, primarily affecting the mitral valve. Early valvular damage leads to regurgitation, with potential progression to leaflet thickening, scarring, calcification, and stenosis over years. Echocardiography is essential for diagnosing asymptomatic RHD.
keyPoints: Up to 75% of ARF patients progress to RHD.
Mitral valve involvement is almost always present; aortic valve may also be affected.
Early valvular damage leads to regurgitation, with potential progression to stenosis over time.
Echocardiography is crucial for diagnosing asymptomatic RHD.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: JOINT INVOLVEMENT | CHOREA
items.title: Polyarthritis in Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF) is migratory and responsive to NSAIDs
items.content: In ARF, polyarthritis typically presents with severe pain affecting large joints such as knees, ankles, hips, and elbows. It is characterized by a migratory pattern where inflammation moves from one joint to another over hours. This form of arthritis responds well to salicylates and other NSAIDs.
keyPoints: Polyarthritis involves hot, swollen, red, and/or tender joints
Affects large joints asymmetrically: knees, ankles, hips, elbows
Migratory pattern moving from one joint to another over hours
Severe pain until anti-inflammatory medication is commenced
Responsive to salicylates and NSAIDs
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · JOINT INVOLVEMENT | CHOREA

items.subtopic: JOINT INVOLVEMENT | CHOREA
items.title: Sydenham's Chorea in ARF requires echocardiography due to high risk of carditis
items.content: Sydenham's chorea, a manifestation of ARF, often occurs without other symptoms and follows a latent period after streptococcal infection. It is more common in females and affects the head and upper limbs with characteristic movements. Over 50% of patients with chorea have carditis, necessitating echocardiography as part of their evaluation.
keyPoints: Chorea may occur without other ARF manifestations
Follows a prolonged latent period after group A streptococcal infection
More common in females; affects head and upper limbs
Characterized by darting tongue movements, hemi-chorea possible
Emotional lability or obsessive-compulsive traits may accompany
Choreiform movements usually resolve within 6 weeks to 6 months
Over 50% of patients with chorea have carditis; echocardiography recommended
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · JOINT INVOLVEMENT | CHOREA

items.subtopic: SKIN MANIFESTATIONS | Stage A: Minimal Echocardiographic Criteria for RHD | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age
items.title: Recognizing ARF Skin Manifestations and Initial Echocardiographic Findings
items.content: Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF) can present with distinctive skin manifestations such as erythema marginatum, which is characterized by pink macules that clear centrally, leaving a serpiginous edge. These rashes typically appear on the trunk and limbs but not on the face. Subcutaneous nodules are another manifestation, presenting as painless lumps beneath the skin over bony prominences like hands, feet, elbows, occiput, and occasionally vertebrae, appearing 2–3 weeks after disease onset.
keyPoints: Erythema marginatum: pink macules with a serpiginous edge; appears on trunk/limbs, not face.
Subcutaneous nodules: painless lumps (0.5–2 cm) over bony prominences; appear 2-3 weeks post-onset.
Nodules associated with carditis and last up to 3 weeks.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SKIN MANIFESTATIONS | Stage A: Minimal Echocardiographic Criteria for RHD | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age

items.subtopic: SKIN MANIFESTATIONS | Stage A: Minimal Echocardiographic Criteria for RHD | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age
items.title: Echocardiographic Criteria for Rheumatic Heart Disease Progression Risk Assessment
items.content: The echocardiographic criteria for assessing the risk of progression in rheumatic heart disease (RHD) vary by age. For individuals aged ≤20 years, mild mitral or aortic regurgitation without morphologic features indicates potential risk. In those >20 years, moderate to severe valvular issues like MR, AR, MS, AS, pulmonary hypertension, and decreased LV systolic function signify high risk of complications requiring intervention.
keyPoints: Aged ≤20: mild MR or AR without morphologic features suggests risk.
Aged >20: at least two morphologic features with mild regurgitation indicate moderate/high risk.
High-risk echocardiographic features include moderate/severe MR, AR, MS, AS, pulmonary hypertension, and decreased LV systolic function.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SKIN MANIFESTATIONS | Stage A: Minimal Echocardiographic Criteria for RHD | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age

items.subtopic: OTHER FEATURES | EVIDENCE OF A PRECEDING GROUP A STREPTOCOCCAL INFECTION | CONFIRMING THE DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Role of Serologic Tests in Diagnosing ARF
items.content: In diagnosing acute rheumatic fever (ARF), serologic evidence is crucial due to the frequent absence of positive throat swab cultures or rapid antigen tests. The anti-streptolysin O (ASO) and anti-DNase B (ADB) titers are commonly used serologic tests.
keyPoints: Serologic evidence usually needed for ARF diagnosis
Common serologic tests: ASO and ADB titers
Age-specific reference ranges should be determined locally
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · OTHER FEATURES | EVIDENCE OF A PRECEDING GROUP A STREPTOCOCCAL INFECTION | CONFIRMING THE DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: OTHER FEATURES | EVIDENCE OF A PRECEDING GROUP A STREPTOCOCCAL INFECTION | CONFIRMING THE DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Jones Criteria for ARF Diagnosis in Different Risk Populations
items.content: The diagnosis of ARF relies on the Jones criteria, which vary based on population risk. Low-risk populations have an incidence ≤2 per 100,000 school-age children or rheumatic heart disease prevalence ≤1 per 1000 annually. In moderate- to high-risk settings, polyarthralgia can be a major manifestation after excluding other causes.
keyPoints: Jones criteria used for ARF diagnosis
Low-risk populations: ARF incidence ≤2/100,000 school-age children or rheumatic heart disease prevalence ≤1/1000 annually
Polyarthralgia considered as major in moderate- to high-risk populations after exclusion of other causes
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · OTHER FEATURES | EVIDENCE OF A PRECEDING GROUP A STREPTOCOCCAL INFECTION | CONFIRMING THE DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | ANTIBIOTICS | TABLE 371-3 Testing and Monitoring of ARF in the Acute Setting Investigations
items.title: Why Echocardiography is Essential for ARF Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Echocardiography should be performed on all patients with possible acute rheumatic fever (ARF) to aid in diagnosis and assess the severity of carditis at baseline. This imaging helps guide treatment decisions, especially when managing heart failure associated with severe carditis.
keyPoints: Echocardiogram is essential for diagnosing ARF and assessing carditis severity.
Baseline echocardiography aids in determining appropriate management strategies.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | ANTIBIOTICS | TABLE 371-3 Testing and Monitoring of ARF in the Acute Setting Investigations

items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | ANTIBIOTICS | TABLE 371-3 Testing and Monitoring of ARF in the Acute Setting Investigations
items.title: How to Treat Group A Streptococcal Infection in ARF Patients
items.content: All patients diagnosed with acute rheumatic fever should receive antibiotics targeting the precipitating group A streptococcal infection. Penicillin is preferred, administered either orally as phenoxymethyl penicillin (500 mg twice daily for adults or 250 mg for children ≤27 kg) or as a single intramuscular dose of benzathine penicillin G (1.2 million units for adults or 600,000 units for children ≤27 kg).
keyPoints: Penicillin is the drug of choice for treating group A streptococcal infection in ARF.
Oral phenoxymethyl penicillin: 500 mg twice daily (250 mg for children ≤27 kg) for 10 days.
Intramuscular benzathine penicillin G: Single dose of 1.2 million units (600,000 units for children ≤27 kg).
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | ANTIBIOTICS | TABLE 371-3 Testing and Monitoring of ARF in the Acute Setting Investigations

items.subtopic: SALICYLATES AND NSAIDS | CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE
items.title: Use of Salicylates and NSAIDs in Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF)
items.content: Salicylates, particularly aspirin, are used to manage arthritis, arthralgia, and fever in ARF. Aspirin is given at 50–60 mg/kg per day up to a maximum of 80–100 mg/kg per day, with monitoring for toxicity symptoms like nausea or tinnitus. Naproxen is preferred by some clinicians due to fewer gastrointestinal side effects and twice-daily dosing.
keyPoints: Aspirin dose: 50–60 mg/kg/day up to 4–8 g/d in adults
Monitor for salicylate toxicity at higher doses (nausea, vomiting, tinnitus)
Naproxen preferred by some due to safety and dosing convenience
Reduce anti-inflammatory medication after 2 weeks of symptom resolution
Recurrence of symptoms up to 3 weeks post-discontinuation does not indicate recurrence
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SALICYLATES AND NSAIDS | CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE

items.subtopic: SALICYLATES AND NSAIDS | CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE
items.title: Glucocorticoids in Severe Carditis Associated with ARF
items.content: The use of glucocorticoids for carditis in ARF is controversial. While older studies showed no benefit, recent data suggest corticosteroids may improve parameters in carditis. Prednisone or prednisolone at 1–2 mg/kg/day (max 80 mg) can be used for severe cases causing heart failure, but potential benefits must be weighed against adverse effects.
keyPoints: Controversial use of glucocorticoids in ARF carditis
Recent data suggest corticosteroids may improve laboratory and echocardiographic parameters
Prednisone/prednisolone dose: 1–2 mg/kg/day, max 80 mg
Use for severe carditis with heart failure, balancing benefits against adverse effects
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SALICYLATES AND NSAIDS | CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION
items.title: Management and Monitoring Post-ARF Acute Episode
items.content: After the acute episode of ARF resolves, it is crucial to ensure long-term follow-up and adherence to secondary prophylaxis. Patients should be registered in local ARF registries if available and primary care practitioners must be engaged for a follow-up plan before discharge. Monitoring inflammatory markers every 1-2 weeks until normalization (usually within 4-6 weeks) and performing an echocardiogram after one month are essential to assess carditis progression.
keyPoints: ARF lasts on average 12 weeks if untreated, but treated patients can be discharged in 1–2 weeks.
Inflammatory markers should normalize within 4–6 weeks.
Echocardiogram is recommended after one month post-acute episode to check for carditis progression.
Long-term follow-up and secondary prophylaxis are critical to prevent recurrence.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION
items.title: Primary Prevention Strategies for ARF
items.content: The primary prevention of ARF focuses on eliminating risk factors such as overcrowded housing and timely treatment of group A Streptococcus infections. Timely antibiotic treatment (penicillin) within 9 days of sore throat onset can prevent nearly all cases of ARF that would otherwise develop. In resource-poor settings, where microbiologic diagnosis is unavailable, guidelines may recommend treating all sore throats with penicillin or using clinical algorithms to identify higher-risk patients.
keyPoints: Primary prevention involves eliminating risk factors like overcrowded housing.
Timely treatment of group A Streptococcus sore throat with antibiotics within 9 days can prevent almost all ARF cases.
In resource-poor settings, treating all sore throats or using clinical algorithms may be recommended despite potential drawbacks.
Focus on preventing group A Streptococcus skin infections in regions where impetigo is endemic.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: SECONDARY PREVENTION
items.title: Optimal Antibiotic Prophylaxis for ARF and RHD
items.content: Long-term penicillin prophylaxis is crucial to prevent recurrences of Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF) after a group A streptococcal infection. Benzathine penicillin G, administered intramuscularly every 4 weeks, is the preferred antibiotic for secondary prevention.
keyPoints: Benzathine penicillin G dosage: 1.2 million units (or 600,000 units if ≤27 kg) every 4 weeks
Alternative dosing schedules: Every 3 or 2 weeks for high-risk patients
Subcutaneous delivery may offer better pharmacokinetics but is not yet recommended in guidelines
Oral penicillin V (250 mg twice daily) as an alternative, though less effective
Erythromycin (250 mg twice daily) for penicillin-allergic patients
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SECONDARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: SECONDARY PREVENTION
items.title: Determining Duration of Secondary Prophylaxis in ARF and RHD
items.content: The duration of secondary prophylaxis for ARF is influenced by the time since the last episode, patient age, and severity of Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD). A coordinated RHD control program with a registry can enhance adherence to prophylaxis.
keyPoints: Factors influencing duration: Time since last ARF episode, patient age, severity of RHD
Recommendations: Minimum 10 years post-last episode or until age 21 (whichever is longer)
Importance of registries in improving adherence and identifying defaulters
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SECONDARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: Which factor is primarily responsible for the decline of ARF incidence in industrialized nations during the early twentieth century?
options: Improved living conditions, including less crowded housing and better hygiene.
The introduction of vaccines against group A Streptococcus.
Natural immunity acquired through exposure to group A Streptococcus.
items.explanation: Improved living conditions, particularly less crowded housing and better hygiene, reduced transmission of group A streptococci, leading to a decline in ARF incidence.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: What is the most common cause of acquired heart disease in children in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs)?
items.explanation: Rheumatic heart disease is the most common cause of acquired heart disease in children in LMICs.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: What percentage of ARF cases and RHD deaths occur in developing countries?
items.explanation: Some 95% of ARF cases and RHD deaths now occur in developing countries.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.explanation: Classically, certain M-serotypes (particularly types 1, 3, 5, 6, 14, 18, 19, 24, 27, and 29) were associated with ARF.

items.question: Which antigen is primarily involved in the molecular mimicry theory of rheumatic fever pathogenesis?
items.explanation: The M protein on group A streptococcus is thought to be involved in molecular mimicry, leading to cross-reactivity with human tissues.

items.question: Which serologic tests are commonly used to provide evidence of a preceding group A streptococcal infection in acute rheumatic fever (ARF)?
options: Anti-streptolysin O (ASO) and anti-DNase B (ADB) titers
Throat swab culture and rapid antigen test
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP)
items.explanation: ASO and ADB titers are the common serologic tests used when throat swab culture or rapid antigen test is negative.

items.question: In diagnosing ARF, what must be determined about the patient's population setting?
options: The incidence of polyarthralgia in the population
Whether the population has a low rate of ARF
The prevalence of subclinical carditis in the population
The frequency of erythema marginatum in the population
items.explanation: Clinicians must determine if the patient is from a setting with known low rates of ARF to apply appropriate diagnostic criteria.

CRP value must be greater than the upper limit of normal

items.explanation: A CRP value greater than the upper limit of normal is required for confirming ARF.

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION
items.question: What is the primary focus after resolving an acute episode of Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF)?
options: Ensuring long-term clinical follow-up and adherence to secondary prophylaxis.
Immediate discharge from hospital without further monitoring.
Starting a new course of antibiotics immediately.
items.explanation: The priority is ensuring long-term follow-up and adherence to secondary prophylaxis after the acute episode has resolved.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION
items.question: What is considered the mainstay of primary prevention for ARF?
options: Development of a vaccine against group A Streptococcus.
Timely and complete treatment of group A streptococcal sore throat with antibiotics.
items.explanation: The mainstay of primary prevention is the timely and complete treatment of group A streptococcal sore throat with antibiotics.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION
items.question: Why might antibiotic overuse be a concern in some settings for ARF prevention?
options: It can lead to increased pressure on antimicrobial resistance.
Antibiotics are ineffective against group A Streptococcus.
Overuse of antibiotics has no drawbacks.
Antibiotic treatment is not recommended for sore throat.
items.explanation: Antibiotic overuse can lead to increased pressure on antimicrobial resistance and other side effects.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: SECONDARY PREVENTION
items.question: What is the recommended antibiotic for secondary prophylaxis in patients with ARF?
items.explanation: Benzathine penicillin G is the best antibiotic for secondary prophylaxis in ARF patients.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SECONDARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: SECONDARY PREVENTION
items.question: How often should benzathine penicillin G be administered intramuscularly for secondary prophylaxis under typical conditions?
items.explanation: Benzathine penicillin G is typically administered every 4 weeks for secondary prophylaxis.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SECONDARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: SECONDARY PREVENTION
items.question: Which factor does NOT influence the duration of secondary prophylaxis in ARF patients?
options: Duration since last episode of ARF
items.explanation: The duration of secondary prophylaxis is influenced by the time since the last ARF episode, age, and severity of RHD, but not by gender.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SECONDARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: ARF and RHD have been virtually eliminated in high-income countries due to improved living conditions alone.
items.explanation: The decline was largely attributable to improved living conditions, but also supplemented by antibiotics and better medical care.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: In 2018, WHO member states adopted a Global Resolution on Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease.
items.explanation: WHO member states unanimously adopted a Global Resolution in 2018 to control these diseases.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | ORGANISM FACTORS | HOST FACTORS
items.statement: Recurrent episodes of Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF) are rare in adolescents and young adults.
items.explanation: Recurrent episodes of ARF remain relatively common in adolescents and young adults.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · EPIDEMIOLOGY | ORGANISM FACTORS | HOST FACTORS

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | ORGANISM FACTORS | HOST FACTORS
items.statement: The susceptibility to Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF) is an inherited characteristic.
items.explanation: Findings of familial clustering and concordance in monozygotic twins confirm that susceptibility to ARF is an inherited characteristic.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · EPIDEMIOLOGY | ORGANISM FACTORS | HOST FACTORS

items.subtopic: THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT
items.statement: Chorea can follow a latent period of up to 6 months after the initial streptococcal infection.
items.explanation: Chorea may have prolonged latent periods lasting up to 6 months.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT
items.statement: Erythema marginatum and subcutaneous nodules are common clinical features in ARF.
items.explanation: These manifestations are now rare, found in less than 5% of cases.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: JOINT INVOLVEMENT | CHOREA
items.statement: Chorea in ARF is characterized by choreiform movements that affect mainly the lower limbs.
items.explanation: The choreiform movements of Sydenham's chorea particularly affect the head and upper limbs.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · JOINT INVOLVEMENT | CHOREA

items.subtopic: JOINT INVOLVEMENT | CHOREA
items.statement: More than 50% of patients with chorea in ARF will have carditis.
items.explanation: More than 50% of patients presenting with chorea will have carditis, necessitating echocardiography as part of the workup.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · JOINT INVOLVEMENT | CHOREA

items.subtopic: SKIN MANIFESTATIONS | Stage A: Minimal Echocardiographic Criteria for RHD | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age
items.statement: Erythema marginatum in ARF commonly appears on the face.
items.explanation: Erythema marginatum almost never occurs on the face.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SKIN MANIFESTATIONS | Stage A: Minimal Echocardiographic Criteria for RHD | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age

items.subtopic: SKIN MANIFESTATIONS | Stage A: Minimal Echocardiographic Criteria for RHD | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age
items.statement: Subcutaneous nodules in ARF are painful.
items.explanation: Subcutaneous nodules are painless.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SKIN MANIFESTATIONS | Stage A: Minimal Echocardiographic Criteria for RHD | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age

items.subtopic: OTHER FEATURES | EVIDENCE OF A PRECEDING GROUP A STREPTOCOCCAL INFECTION | CONFIRMING THE DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: The Jones criteria are used to confirm the diagnosis of ARF.
items.explanation: The Jones criteria help in diagnosing ARF by combining clinical features with evidence of group A streptococcal infection.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · OTHER FEATURES | EVIDENCE OF A PRECEDING GROUP A STREPTOCOCCAL INFECTION | CONFIRMING THE DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: OTHER FEATURES | EVIDENCE OF A PRECEDING GROUP A STREPTOCOCCAL INFECTION | CONFIRMING THE DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: Erythema marginatum and subcutaneous nodules are frequently considered as stand-alone major criteria in ARF diagnosis.
items.explanation: Erythema marginatum and SC nodules are rarely 'stand-alone' major criteria.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · OTHER FEATURES | EVIDENCE OF A PRECEDING GROUP A STREPTOCOCCAL INFECTION | CONFIRMING THE DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | ANTIBIOTICS | TABLE 371-3 Testing and Monitoring of ARF in the Acute Setting Investigations
items.statement: There is a proven treatment for acute rheumatic fever (ARF) that alters the likelihood or severity of developing rheumatic heart disease (RHD).
items.explanation: No treatment has been proven to alter the likelihood or severity of RHD, except for treating heart failure.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | ANTIBIOTICS | TABLE 371-3 Testing and Monitoring of ARF in the Acute Setting Investigations

items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | ANTIBIOTICS | TABLE 371-3 Testing and Monitoring of ARF in the Acute Setting Investigations
items.statement: An echocardiogram is optional when diagnosing acute rheumatic fever.
items.explanation: Echocardiography should be performed on all possible cases to aid diagnosis.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | ANTIBIOTICS | TABLE 371-3 Testing and Monitoring of ARF in the Acute Setting Investigations

items.subtopic: SALICYLATES AND NSAIDS | CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE
items.statement: Glucocorticoids have been conclusively proven to improve the short- and long-term outcomes of carditis in acute rheumatic fever.
items.explanation: Two meta-analyses failed to demonstrate a benefit of glucocorticoids compared to placebo or salicylates in improving the outcome of carditis.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SALICYLATES AND NSAIDS | CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE

items.subtopic: SALICYLATES AND NSAIDS | CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE
items.statement: Fever, joint manifestations, and elevated acute-phase reactants can recur up to 3 weeks after discontinuation of anti-inflammatory medications.
items.explanation: These symptoms sometimes recur up to 3 weeks after medication is discontinued but do not indicate a recurrence.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SALICYLATES AND NSAIDS | CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION
items.statement: Patients with severe carditis require long-term clinical and echocardiographic monitoring.
items.explanation: Severe carditis necessitates close long-term monitoring.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION
items.statement: There is definitive proof that antibiotic treatment of group A streptococcal skin infections prevents ARF.
items.explanation: There is no proof, but increasing evidence suggests a strong association with ARF.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · PROGNOSIS | PRIMARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: SECONDARY PREVENTION
items.statement: Oral penicillin V is more effective than benzathine penicillin G for secondary prophylaxis in ARF.
items.explanation: Oral penicillin V is less effective than benzathine penicillin G for secondary prophylaxis.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SECONDARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: SECONDARY PREVENTION
items.statement: A coordinated RHD control program can improve adherence to secondary prophylaxis.
items.explanation: Registries in a coordinated RHD control program help follow patients and improve adherence.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SECONDARY PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.assertion: The greatest burden of ARF and RHD is found in low- and middle-income countries.
items.reason: These countries often lack the resources, capacity, or interest to tackle these diseases effectively.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why LMICs bear the greatest burden.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · Acute Rheumatic Fever | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT
items.assertion: The initial damage in rheumatic fever is due to molecular mimicry.
items.reason: Streptococcal antigens, particularly M protein, are involved in cross-reactivity with human tissues.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT
items.assertion: Aortic valve involvement is common in rheumatic carditis.
items.reason: The aortic valve is often affected together with the mitral valve.
items.explanation: While the aortic valve can be involved, isolated aortic valve involvement is rare; thus, the assertion is false.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · THE IMMUNE RESPONSE | CLINICAL FEATURES | HEART INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: JOINT INVOLVEMENT | CHOREA
items.assertion: The joint manifestations of ARF are highly responsive to salicylates.
items.reason: NSAIDs, including salicylates, effectively reduce inflammation and pain in affected joints.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the joint manifestations of ARF respond well to salicylates.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · JOINT INVOLVEMENT | CHOREA

items.subtopic: SKIN MANIFESTATIONS | Stage A: Minimal Echocardiographic Criteria for RHD | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age
items.assertion: Erythema marginatum is a classic skin manifestation of ARF.
items.reason: It appears as pink macules that clear centrally, leaving a serpiginous edge.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why erythema marginatum is considered classic.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SKIN MANIFESTATIONS | Stage A: Minimal Echocardiographic Criteria for RHD | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age | Can apply to any age

items.subtopic: SALICYLATES AND NSAIDS | CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE
items.assertion: Glucocorticoids are used in severe carditis cases to reduce acute inflammation and result in more rapid resolution of heart failure.
items.reason: Recent data suggest corticosteroids improve laboratory, radiologic, and echocardiographic parameters in carditis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, the improvement in parameters does not directly explain why clinicians believe glucocorticoids result in more rapid resolution of heart failure.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SALICYLATES AND NSAIDS | CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE

items.subtopic: SECONDARY PREVENTION
items.assertion: Subcutaneous delivery of benzathine penicillin G is recommended for secondary prophylaxis.
items.reason: It provides more optimal pharmacokinetics and reduces pain compared to intramuscular delivery.
items.explanation: Although subcutaneous delivery has advantages, it is not yet recommended in clinical guidelines.
items.reference: Acute Rheumatic Fever · SECONDARY PREVENTION
